summary: Gruber revisits his initial dismissal of Apple's Self Service Repair Program, briefly conceding that it could help independent repair shops procure genuine Apple parts for customers who don't live near authorized service locations. However, after being reminded that Apple's existing Independent Repair Provider Program already serves that purpose, he returns to his original position: the Self Service Repair Program is narrowly targeted at individuals who want to fix their own devices, making it irrelevant to most users.
key_points: Independent repair shops could benefit from access to genuine OEM Apple parts, helping customers far from Apple stores or authorized providers
Apple's repair pricing is often more reasonable than people assume, with examples like a $59 camera replacement and $49 battery swap on six-year-old iPhones
The existing Independent Repair Provider Program (launched 2019) already gives independent shops access to genuine parts, tools, and manuals
The Self Service Repair Program requires submitting a device serial number before receiving parts, unlike the existing program which lets shops stock parts
Gruber ultimately concludes the new program is exactly what it claims to be — for DIY self-repair — and serves only a small sliver of users
money_quotes: The biggest ramification, I think, will be that the program will allow unofficial independent repair shops to procure genuine OEM Apple replacement parts and service manuals.
A lot of the 'Apple's repair policies are screwing people' sentiment is based on the misconception that Apple grossly overcharges for repairs.
$108 total, to return two six-year-old iPhones to perfect working order. As Lipsey noted, that's a tremendous cost-of-ownership value.
Big companies always screw you over for service and repairs; Apple is obscenely profitable and reaps high margins; therefore surely Apple price-gouges for repairs.
I don't see how this week's new Self Service Repair Program helps independent repair shops — or Apple customers who rely on those shops — at all.

The Self Service Repair Program is just what it says on the tin — a program for people who really do want to repair their own devices — and thus is irrelevant to all but a small sliver of actual users.
key_insight: After briefly reconsidering, Gruber concludes Apple's Self Service Repair Program adds little practical value because Apple's existing Independent Repair Provider Program already addresses the more meaningful need for third-party shop access to genuine parts.
